title: How to Obtain an 'Individual Tax Number' (ITN) in South Africa

author.name: Elena Petrova
author.bio: An international business lawyer by trade, Elena breaks down the complexities of freelance contracts, corporate structures, and international liability. Her goal is to empower freelancers with the legal knowledge to operate confidently.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: Understand that obtaining a tax number does not automatically make you a tax resident; your worldwide income is only exposed if you meet specific legal residency tests.
Proactively defend your non-resident status by meticulously tracking your days in-country to stay below the Physical Presence Test thresholds (91/915 days).
During the online registration, use your verified foreign residential address and provide clear proof to firmly establish your non-resident claim with the revenue service.
Immediately after receiving your tax number, log in to your SARS eFiling profile to verify your status is correctly listed as 'non-resident' and correct any administrative errors.
summary: Global professionals risk exposing their worldwide income to South African tax by confusing the administrative act of registration with the legal status of tax residency. To mitigate this, you must first definitively establish your non-resident status using the Physical Presence Test *before* applying, and then meticulously prove this with a complete digital dossier during the online process. Following this strategic approach allows you to operate with confidence, securing a tax number for local compliance while protecting your global earnings from unintended liability.
ai_overview_answer: To register for a South African tax number as a foreign professional, you must apply remotely through the South African Revenue Service (SARS) eFiling online portal. Crucially, obtaining a tax number does not automatically make you a tax resident; residency is determined separately by objective measures like the Physical Presence Test. Before applying, you must assemble a "digital dossier" with a certified passport copy, proof of your foreign residential address, and relevant contracts to substantiate your non-resident status. During the online application, it is essential to use your verified foreign address, not a temporary local one. After receiving your number, your first action must be to log in and confirm your status is correctly listed as "non-resident" to avoid accidental worldwide tax liability. As a non-resident, you are only taxed on income from a South African source but may still be required to file an annual tax return to remain compliant.

step.name: Phase 1: The Pre-Application Framework—Establish Your Status

itemListElement.name: Master the Core Distinction
itemListElement.text: Understand that registering for an individual tax number and becoming a tax resident are two separate events. Non-residents are taxed only on income from a South African source.

itemListElement.name: Run the Residency Tests
itemListElement.text: Use the Physical Presence Test to determine your residency status by tracking your days in South Africa. You are a resident if you are present for more than 91 days in the current and each of the five preceding tax years, AND more than 915 days in total during those five years.

itemListElement.name: Identify Your Scenario
itemListElement.text: Determine your professional profile (e.g., Foreign Freelancer, Expat on Contract, Digital Nomad) to understand your specific reason for needing a tax number and anticipate SARS's expectations.

step.name: Phase 2: The Registration Protocol—Execute with Precision

itemListElement.name: Assemble Your 'Non-Resident Digital Dossier'
itemListElement.text: Prepare a complete set of digital documents including a certified copy of your passport, proof of foreign residential address, a verification selfie, supporting contracts, and banking details.

itemListElement.name: Navigate the eFiling Portal Like a Pro
itemListElement.text: Use the SARS eFiling system to apply. Crucially, use your verified foreign address as your residential address to avoid confusion about your residency status.

itemListElement.name: Anticipate and Overcome Roadblocks
itemListElement.text: If your application is rejected or you face technical issues, book a virtual appointment with a SARS agent to resolve the issue directly.

itemListElement.name: Verify Your Status Immediately
itemListElement.text: Upon receiving your tax number, log in to your SARS eFiling profile to confirm your status is correctly listed as 'non-resident' to prevent your worldwide income from being taxed.

itemListElement.name: Understand Your Filing Obligations
itemListElement.text: Be prepared to file a tax return (even a 'nil return') if required, as holding a tax number may obligate you to do so to avoid penalties.

itemListElement.name: Master Cross-Border Invoicing
itemListElement.text: Update your invoices to include your full name, foreign address, and your new South African tax number to legitimize transactions.

itemListElement.name: Maintain Impeccable Records
itemListElement.text: Keep your details, especially your foreign residential address, updated on your SARS eFiling profile to maintain your non-resident claim.

mainEntity.name: Does getting a SARS tax number make me a tax resident of South Africa?

acceptedAnswer.text: Emphatically, no. Obtaining a tax number does not, by itself, confer tax residency. Residency is determined by the specific criteria detailed in Phase 1. Your tax number is an administrative tool; the *status* associated with that number is what dictates your obligations.

mainEntity.name: Do I need a South African tax number as a foreign freelancer?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, if you are earning income from a South African source. Your client needs your tax number for their own reporting, and having one legitimizes your services, preventing payment delays or withholding complications.

mainEntity.name: Can I register for a tax number from outside the country?

acceptedAnswer.text: Absolutely. The SARS eFiling portal is designed for remote registration. By assembling a complete digital dossier—especially with proof of your foreign address—you can complete the entire process online.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between a tax number for a resident vs. a non-resident?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 10-digit number itself is identical. The critical difference is the status SARS links to it. A 'resident' status means liability on worldwide income; a 'non-resident' status means liability only on South African-sourced income. This is why you must verify your status immediately after registration.

mainEntity.name: How long does it take to get a SARS tax number as a foreigner?

acceptedAnswer.text: With a clear and correct online application, SARS can issue a number within a few working days. However, be prepared for it to take up to 21 days, especially during peak season or if documents require extra verification. Meticulous preparation is the best way to expedite the process.

mainEntity.name: What should I do if I lose my South African tax number?

acceptedAnswer.text: You can easily retrieve it without re-registering through your SARS eFiling profile, by using the 'What's My Tax Number' tool on the SARS website, or by checking employer documents like an IRP5 or payslip.

mainEntity.name: Can my employer register for a tax number on my behalf?

acceptedAnswer.text: While an employer can facilitate the process, the ultimate responsibility for the accuracy of the information rests with you. To protect your non-resident status, it is always best to manage the registration yourself. This gives you complete control over the information submitted to SARS from the very beginning.

body.content: For the elite professional operating across borders, ambiguity is a liability. When it comes to South African tax obligations, the goal isn't just compliance—it's control. Missteps, often born from a misunderstanding of a single concept, can lead to significant financial risk and unnecessary anxiety.

This playbook is designed to eliminate that ambiguity. We will move beyond the mere mechanics of an application and establish a strategic framework for your engagement with the South African Revenue Service (SARS). By following this three-phase guide, you will shift from a position of uncertainty to one of quiet confidence, ensuring your financial foundation in the country is as robust as your professional reputation.
Phase 1: The Pre-Application Framework—Establish Your Status

Before a single document is uploaded, we must address the critical question that dictates your entire relationship with SARS: are you a tax resident or a non-resident? Answering this with precision *before* you apply is the key to mitigating risk. Your long-term obligations are determined by your residency status, not by the mere existence of a tax number.
Master the Core Distinction

Let's be perfectly clear: registering for an individual tax number and becoming a tax resident are two separate events. This distinction is the bedrock of your compliance strategy. South Africa operates on a residence-based tax system, meaning residents are taxed on their worldwide income. In stark contrast, non-residents are taxed *only* on income from a South African source. Securing a tax number as a non-resident is a necessary administrative step for specific local transactions; it does not automatically subject your global earnings to SARS.
Run the Residency Tests

SARS uses two primary tests to determine tax residency. The first is the "ordinarily resident" test, which is subjective and considers where your true home is. For most global professionals, the more relevant measure is the objective **Physical Presence Test**. This is a strict day-counting exercise that serves as your first line of defense against accidental residency.
You are considered a tax resident under this test if you are physically present in South Africa for more than:

items: **91 days** in the current tax year, **AND**
**91 days** in *each* of the five preceding tax years, **AND**
**915 days** in total during those five preceding tax years.

body.content: You must meet **all three** of these requirements to be deemed a resident under this test. Diligently tracking your days in the country is a non-negotiable discipline.
Identify Your Scenario
Your specific reason for needing a tax number clarifies your obligations and allows you to operate with confidence. Pinpoint your profile to anticipate SARS’s expectations.

header: Reason for Needing a Tax Number
rows: Your South African client requires it to process invoices and meet their own tax obligations.
You register as a non-resident to declare only your South African-sourced income. You will likely need to register as a provisional taxpayer.
Your employer requires it for Pay-As-You-Earn (PAYE) tax deductions from your salary while working in South Africa.
You will almost certainly become a tax resident for the duration of your contract. If your employer is not registered with SARS, you must handle your own provisional tax payments.
You are in the country for an extended period, potentially triggering the physical presence test if not carefully managed.
Proactively registering for a tax number while staying under the 91/915-day thresholds ensures you can legally earn from local sources, if needed, without becoming a full tax resident.

body.content: Phase 2: The Registration Protocol—Execute with Precision

With your non-resident status confidently established, the application process transforms from a source of anxiety into a clear, manageable project. This protocol is designed for remote applicants, ensuring you can navigate the SARS eFiling portal smoothly and submit an application that is correct from the first click.
Assemble Your 'Non-Resident Digital Dossier'

Before visiting the SARS website, prepare a complete, high-quality set of digital documents. A poorly scanned passport or a missing proof of address is the most common—and easily avoidable—reason for delays. Think of this as your compliance package, designed to preemptively answer any questions about your non-resident status.
Your dossier must include:

items: **Certified Copy of Your Passport:** Ensure the certification is recent and the entire document, including your photo and personal details, is perfectly legible.

**Proof of Foreign Residential Address:** This is non-negotiable. A recent utility bill or bank statement showing your name and physical address outside of South Africa directly substantiates your claim of non-residency.

**A "Holding Page" Selfie:** SARS requires a unique verification step. Take a clear photograph of yourself holding your original passport. On a separate page, write 'Update my details,' sign it, and date it. Take another selfie holding this signed page. This protects your identity.

**Supporting Contracts:** Include a copy of your employment contract or freelance agreement with the South African entity paying you. If applicable, also include your valid South African work visa or permit.
**Banking Details:** Provide proof of your South African bank account. If you do not have one, a signed letter stating this fact is required.

body.content: Navigate the eFiling Portal Like a Pro
The SARS eFiling system is robust, but it was not primarily designed with the nuances of a foreign national in mind. Knowing the potential friction points is key to a successful submission.

items: **Declare Your Address Correctly:** When prompted for a residential address, you must use your verified *foreign* address. Using a temporary local address can create confusion regarding your residency status.

**Submit Your Dossier:** The system will create a case and prompt you to upload your supporting documents. Ensure each file is a high-resolution scan, clearly labeled to avoid misinterpretation. A blurry or incomplete upload will almost certainly result in a rejection.

body.content: Anticipate and Overcome Roadblocks

Even with meticulous preparation, the most frequent roadblock is a rejection due to SARS being unable to verify your details remotely. If this happens, or if you encounter persistent technical issues, your next step is to book a virtual appointment.

You can schedule a telephonic or video call with a SARS agent to resolve the issue directly. This allows you to bypass system friction and engage with a human expert, ensuring your application gets the attention it deserves. Have your digital dossier ready to be submitted electronically to resolve verification issues quickly.
Phase 3: The Post-Registration Compliance Checklist

Receiving your 10-digit tax number is not the finish line; it is the official start of your compliant relationship with SARS. Your first 90 days are critical for setting a precedent of meticulous control and protecting the non-resident status you worked carefully to establish.
Verify Your Status Immediately

Your first action upon receiving your tax number is to log in to your SARS eFiling profile and confirm your registered status is listed as **"non-resident."** An administrative error could incorrectly classify you as a "resident," exposing your worldwide income to potential taxation. If you find a discrepancy, you must submit a formal request to have it corrected immediately, providing your digital dossier again as proof.
Understand Your Filing Obligations

As a non-resident, your tax duty is tied to income sourced from within South Africa. However, holding a tax number means you may be required to file a tax return even if you earned no local income during a tax year (a "nil return"). Failing to file when required can result in penalties and complicate your compliance record. Staying compliant, even with a zero-income declaration, is essential for maintaining a clean slate.
Master Cross-Border Invoicing
With your tax number in hand, your invoicing protocol must be updated. This small administrative step is a powerful signal of your professionalism.

rows: Clearly display your full name, foreign business address, and your new South African tax number.
This legitimizes the invoice and provides your client with the necessary details for their own tax records.
Include the full legal name and address of the South African entity you are billing.
Accuracy ensures there is no ambiguity for payment processing and tax auditing on their end.
Provide a clear, detailed description of the services rendered.
This substantiates the source of the income and the nature of the cross-border transaction.

body.content: Maintain Impeccable Records

Treat your SARS eFiling profile as a living document. The most critical detail to maintain is your **foreign residential address**, which is the cornerstone of your non-resident claim. Should you move, updating your details on the portal must be a priority. Consistent, accurate record-keeping is your best defense against future compliance complications.
Frequently Asked Questions

items:
**Does getting a SARS tax number make me a tax resident of South Africa?**
    Emphatically, **no**. Obtaining a tax number does not, by itself, confer tax residency. Residency is determined by the specific criteria detailed in Phase 1. Your tax number is an administrative tool; the *status* associated with that number is what dictates your obligations.

**Do I need a South African tax number as a foreign freelancer?**
    Yes, if you are earning income from a South African source. Your client needs your tax number for their own reporting, and having one legitimizes your services, preventing payment delays or withholding complications.

**Can I register for a tax number from outside the country?**
    Absolutely. The SARS eFiling portal is designed for remote registration. By assembling a complete digital dossier—especially with proof of your foreign address—you can complete the entire process online.

**What is the difference between a tax number for a resident vs. a non-resident?**
    The 10-digit number itself is identical. The critical difference is the **status** SARS links to it. A "resident" status means liability on worldwide income; a "non-resident" status means liability only on South African-sourced income. This is why you must verify your status immediately after registration.

**How long does it take to get a SARS tax number as a foreigner?**
    With a clear and correct online application, SARS can issue a number within a few working days. However, be prepared for it to take up to 21 days, especially during peak season or if documents require extra verification. Meticulous preparation is the best way to expedite the process.

**What should I do if I lose my South African tax number?**
    You can easily retrieve it without re-registering through your SARS eFiling profile, by using the "What's My Tax Number" tool on the SARS website, or by checking employer documents like an IRP5 or payslip.

**Can my employer register for a tax number on my behalf?**
    While an employer can facilitate the process, the ultimate responsibility for the accuracy of the information rests with you. To protect your non-resident status, it is always best to manage the registration yourself. This gives you complete control over the information submitted to SARS from the very beginning.

body.content: Conclusion: Operate with Confidence, Not Anxiety

Taking direct control of your tax registration is the final step in shifting your mindset from compliance anxiety to operational confidence. By following this playbook, you have done far more than acquire a 10-digit number. You have strategically engineered a compliant financial foundation that protects your non-resident status and empowers you to engage with the South African market on your own terms.

You began by mastering the critical distinction between residency and registration, neutralizing the single greatest risk: accidental worldwide tax liability. With that foundational clarity, the application process became a manageable project, not a bureaucratic hurdle. Finally, you equipped yourself with a post-registration checklist, recognizing that the number is the beginning of a professional relationship with the revenue service.

You now possess the tools to operate with the profound peace of mind that comes from knowing your financial affairs are in impeccable order. The anxiety is gone, replaced by the quiet confidence of a global professional who has left nothing to chance.
